posts.cooked:
Jancis has a fairly interesting article:
Robinson/Terroir ,
speculating that some winemakers maybe could make a better if they blended their vnyd wines, rather than bottle them separately as vnyd-designate wines.
She details her interview w/ RajParr on his Dom. de la Cote wines. His one large vnyd has 4 rather different vnyds. Raj makes all the wines exactly the same way in an attempt to understand the 4 different terroirs. Jancis speculates that he could, possibly, modify his winemaking for each vnyd and make a better wine. Or even blending all 4 vnyds to yield a better wine.
Whether such would be the case is pure speculation. My suspicion is that the differences are probably pretty subtle. So Raj can charge us more for his experimentation and attempt to understand his terroir.
Tom

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked: Jancis worships at the Altar of Bordeaux, which is all about blending and the art of the blender, so what would you expect?
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.cooked:
J a y H a c k:

Jancis worships at the Altar of Bordeaux, which is all about blending and the art of the blender, so what would you expect?

Yeah, Jay…she pointed out that Bdx doesn’t have the fixation on terroir that they do in Burgundy.
Tom

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked: Perhaps they should blend all the Grand Cru sites in Vosne-Romanee into 1 wine. The total is only 27 hectares. Why waste the time with the six sites when it could all just be one big hodge-podge? So much more of the same wine for everyone!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The differences are quite dramatic, and I’m not clear what one would modify in each case to make the wines ‘better’.

That said, I think she has a point, it’s just a bad example.

I do taste quite a lot of wines from the USA and also from Burgundy where each different vineyard designate bottling feels less complete than the sum of the parts might be. And sometimes that’s borne out by impromptu blends.

Drouhin’s practice of blending their Chambolle 1ers except for Les Amoureuses and their Vosne 1ers except for Petit Monts, for example, makes sense, especially given the nature of their holdings / purchases.

And a heretical suggestion: if Fourrier blended all his 1er crus except for CSJ, how great would the ensuing blend be?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
TomHill:
J a y H a c k:

Jancis worships at the Altar of Bordeaux, which is all about blending and the art of the blender, so what would you expect?

Yeah, Jay…she pointed out that Bdx doesn’t have the fixation on terroir that they do in Burgundy.
Tom

The Bordelais have been trying to talk about it more recently, no doubt realizing that they have lost a rhetorical advantage.

Except for some of them it’s a lot harder when you have a vineyard on a huge piece of pretty flat land.

Jancis has a point though - if you were to take things to the logical extreme, each vine would be vinified separately. Clearly that’s not feasible, but vineyard holdings can be pretty random. Sometimes people know that a group of vines at the top of a slight rise ripen a little differently from those just a few yards away, and maybe those are worth treating differently, but one reason people in the old days planted mixed varieties, aside from the fact that perhaps sometimes they didn’t know they were, is that the blends allowed them to make better wine and to make wine each vintage.

Can’t wait to see her article on the obsession with clones. Monovarietal monoclonal agriculture is the way it’s done now.

posts.name: Michael Sterling
posts.cooked:
It doesn’t have to be either or, does it? Can’t winemakers showcase each individual vineyard’s characteristics (to their best ability) and also make a blend to showcase those combined characteristics as well? I just don’t think it has to be so manichean.

Or not, you know. Showcasing specific vineyard qualities may be all that a particular winemaker is interested in while another may be in love with the potential for blending all sorts of vineyards. It’s a big world, lots of room for everyone

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

And a heretical suggestion: if Fourrier blended all his 1er crus except for CSJ, how great would the ensuing blend be?

If you want some real heresy- now that is nearly 10 years after the event, I think I can safely confess to a little experiment that was conducted among 3 lingering dinner guests at the end of a tasting I put on in 2008.

The central event of the tasting was the entire range of 2006 Domaine de la Romanee-Conti, from the Vosne to Romanee-Conti itself, and Montrachet. There were a couple of other flights as well, so while we did not get crazy drunk, but by the end of the evening we were all feeling a little adventurous.

And there was just a bit left of each of the DRC wines- and so we did what virtually all of us have wondered at some point or another, if we are being honest with ourselves.

We got a measuring cup from the restaurant kitchen and made a DRC blend consisting of equal parts Echezeaux, Grands-Echezeaux, Richebourg, Romanee St. Vivant and La Tache. Adding the Vosne seemed counterproductive, and even in our inebriated state we did not dare add Romanee-Conti.

The result was a rather intoxicatingly spicy blend with a broad array of aromas and flavors. But, it was muddled in a sense- too much really. While it had a lot going on, it did not have anything resembling the dignity and finesse of Romanee-Conti, aka the “perfect sphere” of all things Vosne. Clearly, something was lost in the blending.

Looking at it from the other side- 2012 Dunn Howell Mountain is a very nice wine and the 2012 Trailer Vineyard is a really exotic and interesting creature, but almost too much of a good thing (the 2013 is reputedly over the top altogether). Much as I like the Trailer and look forward to tracking it over time, I think in the grand scheme of things that a blended 2012 Howell Mountain would have been a better idea.

Or- crossing vintages- consider the greatness of Vega Sicilia NV Riserva or what Krug Grand Cuvee can do.

Point being- I think there is value in considering either more or less specificity, and that it really depends on the actual situation. A whole other realm of variables to consider.

Or not, you know. Showcasing specific vineyard qualities may be all that a particular winemaker is interested in while another may be in love with the potential for blending all sorts of vineyards. It’s a big world, lots of room for everyone

well said. Was thinking the exact same thing.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: The problem with Jancis using Domaine de la Cote as her example is that the wines just are not that good. If the wines were actually good and worth their price it would be a different story.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked: What a good idea! One could do a Ghislaine Barthod tasting of her 9 Chambolle premiers crus And a blend of them and look at what you get. Need a bit of patience though

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: I met a chap who had done several experiments with blending DRC wines from cask. Unofficially while working there.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
There are scenarios where skilled (or even perhaps even just competent) blending can make very good wines. Sometimes it’s almost essential, with some blends of different varieties where they work well together.

I know Burlotto do both single vineyard wines and a similarly positioned blend (i.e. not an odds and sods blend of what’s left over, but a proper blend to show what can be done with blends). Bartolo Mascarello’s Barolo famously fought for the tradition of a single blended Barolo.

For Burgundy I sense it’s different, in that this is a region where terroir has long-reigned supreme, and for many enthusiasts, it’s a lifelong quest to really understand the subtleties. For those people blending the wines would be akin to taking their children away, and replacing them with the same number of equally healthy children taken from random homes!

I recall an excellent experiment on the Auswine forum, where someone set up a ‘wine blending night’ where they experimented with blends of (IIRC) 3 reds and 3 whites, to see what the best blend of each colour they could come up with. The answer I recall, was blending isn’t as simple as taking equal parts of each wine. Some have a stronger influence and can dominate at even quite low percentages.

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked:
Good point about equal parts.

Tom may have been happier with a different blend of DRCs.

i think my response to that has to be that when Jancis decides to start making wine, she can blend to her hearts content. until then, the Medoc will continue to blend, and people who are interesting in showcasing different terriors will continue to do so.

Ian Sutton:

There are scenarios where skilled (or even perhaps even just competent) blending can make very good wines. Sometimes it’s almost essential, with some blends of different varieties where they work well together.

I know Burlotto do both single vineyard wines and a similarly positioned blend (i.e. not an odds and sods blend of what’s left over, but a proper blend to show what can be done with blends). Bartolo Mascarello’s Barolo famously fought for the tradition of a single blended Barolo.

For Burgundy I sense it’s different, in that this is a region where terroir has long-reigned supreme, and for many enthusiasts, it’s a lifelong quest to really understand the subtleties. For those people blending the wines would be akin to taking their children away, and replacing them with the same number of equally healthy children taken from random homes!

I recall an excellent experiment on the Auswine forum, where someone set up a ‘wine blending night’ where they experimented with blends of (IIRC) 3 reds and 3 whites, to see what the best blend of each colour they could come up with. The answer I recall, was blending isn’t as simple as taking equal parts of each wine. Some have a stronger influence and can dominate at even quite low percentages.

Yup, Ian…true dat. The wine literature is rife with stories of people, being presented a glass of RedBurg blind, they respond “Ahhh…Richebourg”…or…“Ahhh…Musigny”. I have no reason
to doubt those legendary stories. But those are way beyond my blind tasting skills. And it would cost me big $$$'s to develop those sorts of skills.

Given two glass blind of DRC Echezeaux and Grand Echezeaux, I have little doubt that even I, klutzy palate that I have, could pick up subtle differences.
But to identify which is which…no possible way I could do that…even if I were drinking those wines on a daily or weekly basis. But I’m sure there
are people out there who could do it.

Could DRC blend certain barrels from Echezeaux and Grand Echezeaux and come up with a better wine?? Perhaps they could.
But the people who drink those wines wouldn’t care and it would take away their fun.
Tom

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
TomHill:

Given two glass blind of DRC Echezeaux and Grand Echezeaux, I have little doubt that even I, klutzy palate that I have, could pick up subtle differences.
But to identify which is which…no possible way I could do that…even if I were drinking those wines on a daily or weekly basis. But I’m sure there
are people out there who could do it.

It is a game primarily of memorization and experience. The two are very easy to tell apart- in fact if you were to have just a handful of DRC wines a year for a few years, and a very good memory for them, you might well be able to spot a DRC on nose alone.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Blending is an art. Simply throwing all the different blocks or vineyards you have together is arbitrary. A blend of equal parts of your different lots is also arbitrary. Of course, if the blocks/vyds aren’t particularly distinct, doing separate bottlings can be silly. Sometimes a wine is too good to blend away. Sometimes a wine is wonderful, distinct, but a bit incomplete. Sometimes it has too much of a good thing. Blending complementary wines can make something better than the sum of its parts. Of course, even a single block 100% PN could still be a blend of clones doing that job of complementing each other…

D@vid Bu3ker:

The problem with Jancis using Domaine de la Cote as her example is that the wines just are not that good. If the wines were actually good and worth their price it would be a different story.

Yeah, I wasn’t impressed with what I’ve tried. The idea of doing all the blocks the same sounds a bit, well, Raj Walkerparr. j/k It’s a good starting point, if you pay attention and have the sense how to adjust. But, at the same time, the diving headlong into excessive whole cluster seems like folly, TJ Rodgers style. What I’ve tasted was too much and not well done. Smarter would be to do a small batch of WC from each block, and go from there.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Neal Rosenthal talks about this in his book regarding the trollat wines. I guess Kermit Lynch wanted cuvees from each site and trollat thought (and rosenthal agreed) that they were better as a blend.
